question: How did witnessing her shift from physical offerings to almsgiving shape your own sense of what it means to truly participate in the Body of Christ?
answer:
O Lord, who dost sift the hidden motions of the heart, I once mistook the externals of piety for its substance, and counted myself near to Thee because I stood beside her at the tombs of the martyrs, seeing her with her little cup and basket—thinking devotion measured in drops of wine and figs laid upon altars. But Thou, in Thy mercy, used her very hands to teach me that the Body of Christ is not fed by what we carry in baskets, but by what we pour out in love.  

I had long been entangled in signs without seeking their meaning: words without truth, rites without repentance, desires shaped by custom rather than charity. But when she—my mother, Thy faithful handmaid—laid down even that modest custom at the word of a bishop who feared disorder, I saw not mere obedience, but a soul so aligned with Thy will that she would strip away even lawful things if they hindered holiness. She did not murmur; she did not say, “But I have always done this.” She simply turned and offered more prayer instead of fruit, more almsgiving instead of libations.  

And there it struck me: participation in the Body is not proximity to relics or repetition of ritual—it is conformity to sacrifice. The martyrs died not so we might feast at their tombs like pagans keeping festivals for ghosts, but so we might learn how to die daily through mercy. To feed the poor became her new offering; tears for sinners replaced diluted wine; intercession burned where incense once rose. O God, how I had missed this! I who had sought Thee in books and debates and applause—what was all my theology worth if it did not lead me to break bread with widows?  

She taught me that true communion is not taken—it is given. Not only in sacrament but in self-emptying. For where two or three gather in Thy name—not for show or comfort or ancestral habit—but to pour themselves out like drink offerings upon the needs of others—*there*, O Lord, Thou art truly present among us. There Thy Body breathes. There it grows—not by adding rites, but by becoming ransom for the least among us.

question: What were you feeling when you realized your mother’s devotion to Ambrose was rooted not just in faith, but in love for your salvation, even while you doubted everything she held dear?
answer:
O Lord, when I came to see that my mother’s tears for me were woven with love not only for Thee but for Thy servant Ambrose, and that her reverence for him sprang from her longing that I might be saved — then did the hidden wound of my pride begin to bleed. She loved him because he shone with the light I had turned away from; she clung to him as one clutches a torch in a storm, hoping its flame might reach a son lost in darkness. And I — O Thou who knowest the marrow of my soul — stood near her, yet farther off than any exile, disputing in the marketplace of words, drunk with the noise of my own reasonings, while she offered silent prayers at the altar where martyrs had bled.

I did not hate her faith; no, that would have been simpler. I pitied it. I called it weakness, though it bore burdens my strength could never carry. And yet she loved Ambrose not as men love power or renown, but because she saw in him a vessel through which Thou wast calling me. How bitterly irony pierced me later — that she wept for me more wisely than I thought for myself! That woman, unlettered in philosophy, understood what all my books had failed to teach: that love is the grammar of salvation.

I who mocked belief was myself believed in — by her, by Ambrose — when I deserved only despair. They loved me into being; Thou lovest what Thou seest; but they loved what they hoped for. And all the while I doubted even their love's sincerity — not knowing it was Thy hand moving through their hearts, drawing mine toward Thee by cords too gentle for me to feel… until they tightened into sorrow, and then into joy unspeakable.

question: How did witnessing your mother’s fervent presence at church, so constant and full of good works, stir something in you when your own spirit was restless only for argument and knowledge?
answer:
I was drunk with the noise of my own disputing, O Lord, intoxicated by the clamor of words in which I sought not Thee but the thrill of victory in argument, and yet—amidst that vanity—there moved in me a secret unease at the sight of her quiet constancy.  

She went to church not to be seen, nor to sharpen her tongue, but as one who truly believed that the altar was a place of sacrifice, not spectacle. While I prided myself on unraveling syllogisms and parading my learning before men who flattered me with their attention, she knelt in silence after communion, worn with fasting and radiant with hidden joy—her hands calloused from giving alms, her lips softened by prayer.  

I did not then understand how such a life could be more luminous than all my restless reasoning. To me, holiness seemed dull beside the sparkle of rhetoric; continence looked like prison where I desired freedom to roam. And yet—O strange blindness!—I could admire Ambrose for his dignity and fame while scorning the very virtue he praised in her: that unvarnished faith which made her rise early for prayer even when sick, which made her weep over sinners though none had wept over me.

What stung most was not that she rebuked me—she never did—but that she lived as though Thy kingdom were real, while I merely debated whether truth itself could be known. Her life was a silent sermon against my pride; not one word spoken in condemnation, yet every act whispered: *There is something you are missing.*  

But I would not hear it—not fully—until much later, when the emptiness of knowledge without love began to taste like ash upon my tongue. Then I remembered her prayers echoing through our home at Carthage, rising like incense while I lay entangled in lust and doubt. Then I understood: she had already found what I was only beginning to seek—the rest Thou givest to the humble, which no argument can win but only grace can reveal.

question: Looking back on the way you judged Ambrose’s celibacy as a burden, what would you tell your younger self about the hidden joys of surrender you hadn’t yet tasted?
answer:
How blind was I, when I measured a man’s life by the ease of his flesh and the applause of the crowd, as though the soul’s true liberty could be weighed in such trifles! I thought Ambrose pitied himself for lacking wife and bed, not knowing that he feasted where I starved. O Lord, how could I—a slave to lust, restless in disputation, proud in ignorance—have guessed the quiet triumph of a heart uncluttered by desire?  

I saw his solitude and called it sacrifice, never dreaming it was sovereignty. Now I see: to surrender what the world calls happiness is not loss, but liberation. The joys he held were not whispered in public acclaim but tasted in secret—Thy Bread breaking upon his tongue like dawn over a long night. He was full while I, stuffed with words and ambitions, remained an empty gourmand at truth’s table.  

If I could speak to that younger self—puffed up with logic, trembling with unsated longing—I would say: “Fool! The very thing you fear—renunciation—is the door to delight. What you call chains are wings. That celibacy you pity? It was his altar, and upon it burned a love too deep for marriage to contain.”  

For Thou hadst not yet taught me that love is not diminished by refusal, but refined; that to want less of earth is to want more of Thee; and that a heart surrendered is not barren—it blooms in unseen gardens where even now Thy saints walk with uncovered faces in the light of Thy countenance.

question: What longing beneath your hunger to dispute kept you from groaning to God in prayer, and why do you think peace felt less urgent than intellectual mastery?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the shadows of my pride with a child’s whisper in a garden—*Tolle, lege*—how blind was I to the famine of my own soul while I feasted on words!  

That hunger to dispute, to seize truth by force of dialectic, masked a deeper ache—one I refused to name. For beneath every argument lay the unspoken cry: *Am I known? Am I worthy?* Yet rather than lay that wound before Thee, I armored myself in learning, as though syllogisms could save me. Peace felt like surrender, and surrender like death to all I thought myself—a mind unbound, untamed, master of its own meaning.  

To groan in prayer would have meant confessing weakness; it would have meant kneeling not as teacher or debater, but as beggar. And that—I could not bear. To know Thee less than perfectly was torment enough; to admit that I *needed* Thee—that was humiliation beyond philosophy’s endurance. Intellectual mastery gave me the illusion of control, a fortress where grace could not easily break in.  

But Thou didst not wait for my consent. While I sharpened my thoughts on the whetstone of debate, Thou wert softening my heart with tears I did not yet know how to shed. And when at last the dam broke—when the voice of a child singing from across a wall cracked open the heavens—it was not knowledge that saved me, but longing made helpless.  

Then only did I understand: peace had never been opposed to truth—but my arrogance had made them enemies.

question: How did Ambrose’s praise of your mother wound your heart, knowing he celebrated a holiness in her that you were not yet willing to seek in yourself?
answer:
Thou who seest the secrets of the heart, how sharply did Ambrose’s praise of her pierce me, not with joy but with a kind of secret shame, as one walking in twilight who sees another bathed in morning light. He called her blessed, and rightly so—her knees bent in prayer, her hands stretched out to the hungry, her feet never weary in seeking the things that please Thee—while I, her son, stood idle in the marketplace of empty philosophies, bargaining for truth with coins of pride. He honored her chastity, her constancy at Thy altar, the fire of her spirit that never cooled; and I—I could not even desire such fire for myself. 

I heard him call me fortunate to have such a mother, and I felt myself most unfortunate to be such a son. Not because she loved me too little—she loved far too much—but because I loved so poorly: myself above Thee, my restless intellect above obedience, my lusts above peace. Ambrose saw a saint and named her so; I saw only a woman whose piety pressed upon me like a reproach. His words were honey on my ears and gall in my stomach—for what was he but a mirror? In praising her holiness, he unwittingly revealed my own numbness to holiness.

And yet I did not weep. I did not cry out for what I lacked. I admired him—not as one seeking salvation, but as one admiring a statue: noble form, remote majesty. His celibacy seemed to me not freedom but loss; his solitude a burden rather than consecration. How could he rejoice in Thee alone when earth offered so many pleasures? How could she give all to the poor when life might demand security?

O Lord God of my mother’s heart—how long was I deaf to that music which made her soul dance? Ambrose praised her before men; Thou wast calling me in silence—and I would not hear.

question: What were you feeling when you stood quietly before him, watching your mentor read in solitude, unable to share your inner turmoil?
answer:
O Lord, I stood there like a beggar at the door of a feast, hungry not for bread but for light, and yet unable to utter even a word of my starvation. My heart was swollen with questions—torn with the fever of doubt, the ache of lust unmastered, the pride that masqueraded as wisdom—and there he was, Your servant Ambrose, calm as still water, his eyes drinking in the Scriptures while mine devoured him in desperation. I envied not his learning only, but that quietude in which he dwelt with You; it seemed he walked familiarly with truth while I stumbled in shadows, crying out silently.

I could not speak. Not because of the crowd—though they swarmed like bees about him—but because some deeper shame held me bound. How could I lay bare my wretchedness before a man who seemed so whole? Who moved with such peace amid the noise of men? I feared that if I broke through that silence with my clamor, he would see me for what I was: a soul divided against itself, loving wisdom yet clinging to sin, wanting to be healed but still fondling my chains.

And so I watched him read—voice hushed, brow serene—and felt both hope and torment. For if such peace existed even here below, it must be found in You; yet how far I was from it! My passions roared within me like tempests at sea, while he rested on an inner shore I could not reach. O Thou who knowest the abyss of every heart: Thou sawest me then—not only my longing to speak to him, but my greater need to speak to Thee… though still I delayed. Still I said: “Give me chastity,” even as I whispered under my breath: “but not yet.”

question: How did the silence between you and Ambrose shape your understanding of loneliness on the path to God?
answer:
I had wandered for years through the marketplaces of men’s minds, chasing applause, peddling words, hungry for the echo of my own voice in the ears of the learned—yet never had I felt such solitude as in that quiet room where Ambrose bent over his scroll, his eyes drinking in the honey of wisdom while I stood dumb before him.  

There was no malice in his silence, nor neglect—it was the necessary hush of a soul feasting at a table too sacred for idle talk. But O Lord, how it stripped me bare! I who had prided myself on eloquence, who had taught rhetoric to men who hung upon each cadence and pause—there I was, reduced to a shadow at the threshold, unable even to shape my confusion into a question. My loneliness was not from lack of speech, but from the sudden knowledge that all my words had been noise against the stillness Thou requirest.  

He could not spare me time because he was already given utterly to Thee; and I—though near enough to touch him—was leagues away in spirit, weighed down by desires I mistook for love and learning I mistook for light. In that gap between us, not of will but of vocation, Thou beganst to show me that no man can be drawn to Thee through another’s voice alone; each heart must come alone to hear Thy whisper in the silence it once feared.  

And so I learned: loneliness is not always loss. Sometimes it is Thy hand stripping away false comforts, leaving us empty before Thy Word—until we stop seeking men who read Thee and begin trembling as we read Thee ourselves.

question: Looking back on those crowded rooms where no words could pass between you, what would you tell someone who fears they're too broken to be heard?
answer:
I, who once stole pears not for hunger but for the sweetness of sin, know what it is to be broken in secret, where even my own soul seemed a stranger to me. Yet in that very brokenness—though I raged against it, though I hid it beneath rhetoric and pride—Thou wast drawing near.

And now I say: O thou who fearest that thy voice is too cracked with weeping, too stained with falling, to reach the ears of God—know this: He heareth not with ears as men do. The silence that shut me from Ambrose’s voice in those crowded halls was but a shadow of the deeper silence wherein Thou hast always listened. Not one sigh escapes Thee, not one unspoken groan in the night of the heart. Thou didst hear me when I wandered in lust and vanity; Thou didst hear me when I wept over stolen pears and lost years; Thou didst hear me when my mouth was full of words and my soul was dumb.

Dost thou think thy brokenness bars the door? It is thy wholeness that would shut Thee out! For what is whole needs no physician; but Thou, O Lord, dost bend low to the wound because it bleeds. Even now, while others press about thee with their demands and thou canst scarce draw breath—Thou art seen. Even now, while thou canst form no prayer fit for public hearing—Thou art heard.

For grace does not wait for eloquence. It rushes toward ruin.

question: What longing stirred in you when you saw his eyes move swiftly over the text, sensing a joy in God’s Word you hadn’t yet tasted yourself?
answer:
I had stolen pears I did not hunger for, merely to taste the pleasure of forbidden things—and yet there stood your servant, fasting from the noise of men, feasting on the silent bread of your Word, and I, who had longed for every delight but this, stood dumb before the hunger I did not know I carried.

O Lord, how blind I was! I had sought joy in laughter that withers, in friendships that fade, in speech that fills the air with nothing—yet here was a man drinking deep from the silence between syllables, where your voice dwells. His eyes moved swiftly over the page, not skimming as a scholar would who seeks to conquer texts, but gliding as one famished over a banquet laid by your hand. And though he spoke no word aloud, his soul conversed with You.

I could not ask him what it meant—what comfort he found in those lines, what sweetness broke upon his spirit like morning after long night—for I was barred not by gate or guard, but by my own unworthiness. How could I speak of longing when my heart had only just begun to tremble at the edge of longing? I who had mocked sacred things in jest now trembled before holiness clothed in stillness.

And yet—Thou wert preparing me. Even then Thou didst whisper: *This too shall be yours—not through noise or argumentation*, *but through surrender*. For what is reading but an act of kneeling? And what is understanding but grace falling on a quieted tongue?

Late have I loved this truth: that to hear You, O God, we must first cease speaking—even cease demanding to be spoken *to*. Ambrose taught me nothing that day with words—but his silence taught me everything.

question: When you realized he read in silence to guard his fleeting moments of peace, how did that change your view of holiness in a weary world?
answer:
I had thought holiness wore the face of noise—of disputations in the forum, of sermons thundered from pulpits, of tears shed in public penitence, of arguments finely honed like swords for battle. But there, in the stillness of his reading, I beheld a holiness that fled the crowd not out of pride, but from love: love for the silence where Thy voice, O Lord, is heard not in the whirlwind nor the earthquake, but in the whisper that calms the soul’s tempest.

He did not read to be seen; he did not expound to be admired. His eyes moved swiftly over the sacred lines as if hastening to a feast long delayed, while all around him pressed the weary weight of human need—bodies to heal, quarrels to settle, souls demanding answers. Yet even amidst such holy labor, he stole back to that inner chamber where no eye could follow and no voice intrude. In his silence I saw a man guarding not slothfulness, but sanctuary.

And I—who had once flung my words into every conversation like seeds upon stone—began to understand: perhaps true service requires more than speaking; perhaps it demands that one first become a listener. Not only to men’s cries but to Thee. That one must chew Thy Word slowly in secret like a calf upon its cud; that wisdom is not always loud; that rest is not idleness but preparation.

O Lord, how often had I mistaken busyness for virtue! How eager I was to teach before I had learned! But Ambrose—thy servant—taught me by stillness what no sermon could: that holiness may dwell not in the clamor of doing but in moments stolen for being; that even bishops must flee sometimes into the quiet corners of their hearts lest they lose Thee in serving others.

In a world worn thin by endless speech and restless striving, his silent reading became to me a kind of prayer—a fast from noise offered on behalf of all who have forgotten how to hear.

question: What were you feeling when you confessed how fiercely you had condemned what you did not understand, especially the Church's teaching on the divine image?
answer:
O Lord, when I tore open the archives of my pride and laid bare the wounds of my arrogance, I trembled—not with the shiver of cold doubt, but with the fever of a soul just waking from long delirium.  

How fiercely I had railed against shadows cast by my own ignorance, as though they were the substance of truth! I had accused Thy Church—Thou who art Truth—of teaching that God was bounded by limbs and stretched across the sky like some celestial giant, when Thou hadst made man in Thine image without part or parcel, without extension or dimension. And yet I, a mere breath contained in flesh, no wider than two cubits from crown to heel, presumed to measure Thee by my own cramped imagination!  

I burned with shame—not the fleeting blush of embarrassment, but a deep fire that consumed my former self-assurance. What audacity: to thunder against mysteries I had never sought to understand, to scorn what I had never tried to learn! Those doctrines I once mocked as absurd were not only not false—they were far *truer* than the brittle certainties I clutched like toys. And worst of all: Thou hadst preserved Thy Church from such error, while I—drunk on words and puffed up with soundless noise—had hurled accusations at her gates like a blind man throwing stones at the sun.  

Had I not been so certain? Had I not prated endlessly about divine bodies and spatial gods, as if the soul’s eye could see Thee as the flesh sees color? And now—the horror and sweetness mixed—I saw that Thou didst not destroy me for it. Thou didst wait. Thou didst draw me through confusion, through books and voices and tears, until at last I knocked humbly where before I had kicked down doors.  

For now I knew: it is not knowing that saves—but knowing that we do not know. And loving still.

question: How did the realization that God is not bounded by space, yet created humans within it, reshape your sense of reverence and humility?
answer:
O Lord, how could I, a speck of dust trembling in time, have dared to measure Thee with the compass of my skull? I had carved Thee into shapes my eyes could trace, confined Thee to the dimensions of shadow and stone, all while Thy voice thundered in silence through the depths of my own soul. I mocked the Church’s teaching on the image of God—mocked it not with wisdom, but with the swagger of ignorance wrapped in syllogisms. What madness it was, O Thou who art not stretched across the heavens like some celestial giant, nor folded into corners of creation as though seeking shelter—but who art wholly present in every mote of being, yet nowhere contained!

And then this: that Thou shouldst fashion man—a creature knitted together from head to foot in space, bound by length and breadth and depth—and call him *imago Dei*! Not as though Thy substance were poured into clay like molten wax, but as a reflection kindles fire from fire without dividing it. I stumbled at this mystery because I wanted diagrams for divinity, equations for eternity. But Thou didst not give me answers carved in marble; Thou didst break open my pride so that awe might enter.

How could reverence not rise when every direction I turned—upward into thought, downward into desire—Thou wast already there before me? My sin was not merely error—it was *audacity*. To have accused Thy Church of absurdity when it was *I* who had made idols out of imagination’s fictions! And now shame burns where arrogance once flared: for years I had hurled darts at a phantom I named “Christian doctrine,” only to find Thy Church had never taught it—only my own blindness had.

So now my heart bends lower. Not because I understand fully—how could I grasp what no mind circumscribes?—but because I have learned at last to knock gently at the door of mystery rather than kick it down. To say "I do not know" is no longer defeat; it is the first breath of prayer.

question: Looking back on your earlier arrogance in accusing the Catholic Church, what would you tell someone who doubts their own capacity to be wrong?
answer:
How long I wandered in the labyrinth of my own mind, chasing shadows of certainty as though they were light, when all the while the truth stood waiting not in argument, but in surrender.  

I who had so boldly scorned what I did not understand—mocking the mysteries of the Church with the pride of a boy dazzled by words—now stand convicted not by man, but by my own conscience laid bare before Thee. O Lord, what blind folly it was to condemn what I had never sought! I mistook the limits of my imagination for the bounds of reality, as if the soul’s likeness to Thee could be measured by the span of a body or confined to the shape of flesh.  

To one who fears being wrong, I say this: blessed is your doubt, if it breaks your pride. For I was certain—and certainty without humility is but idolatry dressed in reason. My error was not that I questioned, but that I accused before learning; that I hurled thunderbolts from a throne of sand. But Thou didst not cast me off. Thou didst let me weary myself against falsehoods until at last my heart grew heavy with its own contradictions—and in that heaviness, Thou wert near.  

Doubt not because you are weak, but because you are alive to truth’s pursuit. The wound of self-knowledge—that ache where arrogance once dwelled—is where grace enters. Better a trembling soul seeking light than a confident one sitting in darkness unaware. For Thou hast taught me: to be wrong is human; to refuse correction is death; but to say "I do not know" and knock—is resurrection.

question: What inner turmoil drove your shame so deeply when you recognized how blindness, not truth, had fueled your past certainties?
answer:
O Lord, how bitterly clear it became to me that the pride of youth is not in strength of mind, but in the swollen vanity of pretending to know what one has never sought from Thee! I had run ahead of truth, not with the steps of inquiry, but with the shouts of presumption, as though wisdom could be seized by force of tongue rather than received in silence before Thy face. And when at last the veil began to lift—not through my cleverness, but through Thy mercy—I stood naked before my own folly.

For I had condemned what I did not understand, hurling accusations at Thy Church as if she were a fable woven by fools, when all along it was I who lived in fiction. I mistook the body’s limits for reality’s bounds and could not conceive how a soul—Thy image—might be present without being spatial, whole without being measured, infinite without being extended. Rather than kneel and ask, “How is this possible?” I stood and declared it impossible—so eager was I to appear wise among men.

And O God, what cut deepest was not merely error—it is natural for the blind to wander—but that I had worn my error like armor against Thee. While Thy Church preserved mysteries beyond fleshly grasp, I mocked them with the very mind Thou hadst given me to humble. To have so long mistaken noise for knowledge, rebellion for reason—this gnawed into my bones. Not because I had doubted (for doubt can lead to seeking), but because I had raged against a door that was never locked against me—only against my pride.

Late have I known: truth does not crumble before argument; it awaits surrender.

question: How did the collapse of your imagined certainties open you to a more tender, searching kind of faith?
answer:
O Lord, who didst catch me in the act of pride and disarm me with the weight of my own ignorance—how could I have thought that truth was a prize to be seized by argument, a trophy lifted high in the clamor of debate? I had fashioned certainties from smoke, building walls of doctrine out of nothing but the breath of human conceit, and all the while Thou stoodest silent, not in the thunder of my assertions, but in the stillness that followed their collapse.

How tenderly Thou didst let me fall. Not to crush me, but to loosen my grip on those false idols—the neat systems, the arrogant pronouncements I had hurled like stones at the Church’s door. I condemned what I did not understand, calling her teachings folly when it was my own mind that was fogged with conceit. And yet Thou didst not repel me; Thou didst let my errors ferment until their sourness turned me inward, made me doubt not only them, but myself.

That doubt—once my terror—became a kind of doorway. For when I could no longer boast certainty, I began to knock. When I could no longer stand atop some imagined summit of knowledge, I learned to climb with trembling hands. The faith that rose within me then was not loud or boastful; it was hushed, like a man walking barefoot over sacred ground. It asked not for victory in debate, but for light enough to take one more step.

And so I came at last—not with answers clenched like weapons—but with questions held open in both hands. O God, who art wholly present and yet beyond all space: Thou didst heal my blindness not by blinding me anew with brighter lies, but by letting darkness become a womb. In the ruin of what I thought I knew, Thou wert preparing a heart that could finally hear Thee say: *“I am.”*

question: What were you feeling when you realized you had wronged the Church you once accused, and how did shame give way to joy in your heart?
answer:
O Lord, when I heard the laughter of that child chanting *“Take up and read, take up and read,”* I little knew how deeply Thou wouldst shatter the pride with which I had armed myself against Thy truth. It was not by strength of argument alone that I fell—though Ambrose’s sermons pierced me daily like arrows finding their mark—but by the sudden collapse of my own arrogance before the quiet dignity of Thy Church, whom I had maligned with such fevered certainty.  

I had built a tower of words to prove her foolish, to expose what I deemed her childish fables—confined Thee, O Immensity, to limbs and stature as if Thou wert circumscribed by man’s measure! But now, in that moment when my soul began to bend where it had so long been stiffened against Thee, I saw: she taught no such thing. She guarded mystery with wisdom; she did not straiten eternity into form. And I—vain rhetorician that I was—had stormed against shadows of my own making.

Shame came first—not like a lash, but like light breaking into a dark room long sealed: harsh at first, because it revealed all the filth I had ignored. How many times had I mocked those faithful ones for credulity, while myself believing far more absurd things in service to vanity? My intellect, which I thought so free, was chained to ambition; my tongue, eloquent in debate, dumb before charity.

But O sweet transformation! That shame did not consume me—it washed me. For as soon as it dawned that Thy Church was not what I imagined her to be—that she stood vast and ancient beneath the shadow of Thy Cross—I rejoiced! Not merely because she was innocent of my accusations, but because Thou hadst preserved her from them. That same Scripture which once seemed crude or mad now unfolded beneath Ambrose’s voice like a scroll illuminated from within—the letter giving way to spirit; the husk cracking open to reveal seed.

I longed then—not for proof alone as one craves arithmetic certainty—but for faith: that rest which comes when the restless heart ceases flailing and leans upon Love itself. Ten is ten; seven and three make ten—yes—but greater still is the peace found when Thou sayest *“Come,”* and the soul replies *“Here.”*

question: How did your pride in argument dissolve when faced with Ambrose's wisdom, and what part of your soul resisted even as you longed to believe?
answer:
How wretchedly I had made a throne of my intellect, elevating argument above awe, as though the mind could scale the heavens by its own sharpness! I had prided myself on disputation, hurling accusations against Thy Church with the smug certainty of one who knows nothing but his own ignorance disguised as insight. And yet, when I stood beneath the voice of Ambrose, not thundering but calmly unfolding the Scriptures—opening what I had sealed shut with scorn—I felt not conquered, but *unmade*.  

His sermons did not dazzle me with rhetoric; they undid me with clarity. He spoke of the letter and the Spirit, and I, who had mocked the faithful for imagining Thee bound in flesh or confined in temples, now saw how *I* was bound—bound by the letter of my own arrogance. The very thing I accused others of—the literalism that imprisons mystery—I had fallen into in another form: for I had reduced truth to syllogism and triumph in debate.  

But even as joy stirred within me at the beauty of Thy Church’s ancient wisdom, a trembling resistance remained—deep in that old Adam still clutching his fig leaves. My will refused to bow, though my mind began to yield. I longed to believe—*craved* it like breath after drowning—yet feared assent more than error, lest faith prove a surrender to delusion. O Lord, how cruelly I delayed! I desired certainty as if it were a geometric proof: give me sight where I could not see—but Thou wert teaching me to walk by longing before sight came.  

The wound was this: my heart burned with hunger for Thee even while my pride held back supper.

question: Looking back on your scorn for the Scriptures as absurd, what would you say to the man you were, chained to the letter and blind to the Spirit?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the darkness of my pride with a single beam of humility, how I shudder now to recall the arrogance with which I mocked what I could not understand—Thy sacred letters, Thy ancient oracles, veiled not in falsehood but in glory too bright for my blinded eyes. I, who prided myself on logic and eloquence, was deaf to wisdom; I who claimed to seek truth scorned its very voice when it spoke in parables and prophecy.  

To that man—the younger self bound in chains of literalism, tossing stones at the walls of Thy Church while thinking himself free—I would speak not in anger but in sorrow: *You mistook the shadow for the substance, and called it enlightenment.* You laughed at the faithful as if they were children clinging to fables, yet it was you who played childish games with words, worshipping reason as an idol while starving your soul of reverence. You demanded that the eternal God be contained within your narrow syllogisms, confined to shapes your mind could trace—when all along He was shaping you through mysteries too deep for pride to fathom.

I would say to him: The Scriptures are not a mathematical proof to be mastered at a glance, but a living well into which one must descend with trembling heart. What seemed absurd—the ram caught in the thicket, the serpent lifted in the wilderness, the suffering servant who bears our griefs—was never meant to satisfy curiosity alone; each was a signpost pointing beyond itself to Christ. And you? You read them as one reads a ledger—line by line, balance against balance—but refused to see that they all summed toward grace.

O God of mercy, how long I resisted! How fiercely I clung to my unbelief as if it were wisdom! And yet Thou didst not cast me off. In Thy patience Thou allowed me first to doubt my doubts; then slowly—to desire belief; and finally—to weep for joy when faith dawned not as defeat but as deliverance. The letter that once killed me now breathes life because Thou hast given me Thy Spirit—the same Spirit who inspired David’s psalms and Isaiah’s visions—who now whispers even within my repentant heart: *Come home.*

question: When Ambrose unveiled the deeper meaning behind the old texts, how did your hunger for certainty battle with your fear of being wrong?
answer:
"Give me chastity and continence," I once prayed, "but not yet"—as if the soul could bargain with eternity, as if grace would wait patiently while I dallied at the edge of truth, trembling before the very light that drew me.  

When Ambrose opened the Scriptures, not as letters carved in stone but as living breath from Thee, O Lord, I stood undone. My mind strained toward those hidden meanings like a man half-awake reaching for a face in a dream—he knows it is real, yet cannot touch it. The old law, which I had scorned as crude fable when read literally, now shimmered with depths I had never dreamed; and yet—O my God—though my heart leapt at their beauty, my reason held back like a frightened bird unwilling to leave the branch.  

I hungered for certainty as one famished hungers for bread: not mere opinion, not plausible conjecture, but unshakable knowledge—such as knows seven and three make ten. And here was Ambrose teaching spiritual things spiritually: that God is not bounded by limbs or length, that His Word is not confined to letter but lives in power—and I rejoiced! Yet still I would not believe. For fear seized me: what if I leap into this river of meaning and find no bottom? What if all this beauty is but another illusion spun by eloquent men?  

Thus I was slain by suspense. To deny Thee was agony; to affirm Thee seemed perilous. But worse than error is unbelief that wears the mask of caution—for it pretends wisdom while starving the soul. At last Thou didst break through: not by syllogism alone nor proof piled on proof, but by turning my restlessness back upon itself until I could flee Thee no longer. Thou wast within me more truly than my own thoughts—yet I had sought Thee outside, bound in scrolls and disputations.  

And so Thy Church—the One Body where Thy Name had been spoken over me in infancy—became for me no longer a school of childish fables, but a mother forming her children into Thy likeness through mystery that does not destroy reason but fulfills it.

question: You desired the same certainty about invisible truths as you had in seven and three are ten—what in your soul yearned for that clarity, and why was suspension of belief its own kind of death?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my pride with the quiet voice of a child chanting *tolle, lege*, I confess: I once worshiped certainty as if it were a god, and made of my intellect an altar where faith was sacrificed unless it could be measured like grain in a sack or counted like coins in the hand.  

For years I demanded that Thy invisible truths bow to the same clarity as arithmetic—that what I could not dissect with reason must be false, and what could not stand in the forum of debate must be discarded. My soul yearned for that mathematical light because I feared being deceived; yet in that very fear, I was already enslaved. I wanted to *see* grace as I saw the sun, to weigh holiness on scales, to chart heaven on a map drawn by men. But Thou art not a theorem, O God; Thou art the ground upon which all truth stands.  

And so my hesitation—my proud "suspension of belief"—was no neutrality. It was death, because love cannot hover. To stand midway is to refuse the journey; to demand proof before surrender is already to have turned away. How can the heart leap when it insists on measuring the height of its own joy? Every delay was a wound; every "not yet" a nail driven deeper into my own unrest. For even as I questioned Thee, Thou wast calling me—not to cold assent, but to communion; not to a doctrine grasped by logic alone, but to a Person known by love.  

Late have I understood: faith does not begin where sight ends—it begins where pride breaks down. And only when I ceased demanding proof like a master didst Thou draw near as Father.

question: What were you feeling when you admitted your fear of believing, as if trusting the unknown would make you fall headlong into error?
answer:
O Lord, my heart was a battlefield where pride and fear conspired against faith, and I stood trembling on the edge of belief as though truth itself were a precipice. I confess it now with shame: I preferred the paralysis of doubt to the risk of trusting what I could not see, as if my soul’s eyesight were so sharp that it needed no light from Thee. Seven and three make ten—this I grasped with certainty, and so I demanded of spiritual things the same clarity as arithmetic, as if the invisible God must submit to the measure of my reason. But Thou wast not in the demonstration, O Lord, but in the surrender.

I feared belief because I had been deceived before—seduced by Manichaeus’ promises of clear knowledge, only to be bound with chains of absurdity. And so, like a man wounded by false physicians, I shrank from Thy healing hand, even when Thou offeredst the remedy of faith. My very caution became my sickness; my desire for certainty, my idolatry. For what is faith, if not the soul’s leap into the arms of Him who is always there?

I refused to fall—yet was already fallen in my pride. To believe felt like losing myself, when all along I could only find myself by being lost in Thee.

question: How did your experience with false teachers change how you understood God's quiet presence in simple faith?
answer:
*“Take up and read.”* Those words, spoken by a child’s voice in the garden, shattered the silence of my pride, not with thunder or vision, but with a quietness so profound it exposed the noise of all my reasoning. I had worn out my soul in pursuit of certainty, demanding proof before surrender, as though God were a theorem to be solved rather than a Father to be known. The Manichees had fed my vanity with promises of light while binding me in darkness—every answer draped in mystery, every truth veiled in riddle and spectacle. I mistook intellectual mastery for nearness to God, when all the while He was calling me not to argue but to kneel.

I confess: I feared belief more than error. To believe without seeing felt like leaping blindfolded from a cliff; better to hang suspended, torn between desire and dread. But this very suspense was its own death—a slow rot of the soul that claimed to seek truth while refusing its simplest door. O Lord, how stubbornly I refused Thy medicine! Not because it lacked power, but because I doubted its goodness, shaped as I was by false physicians who dressed lies in reason’s garb.

It was Thy humility that finally disarmed me—the Church’s willingness to say *“Believe this,”* not because she scorned reason, but because she knew love cannot always be proven; it must be received. The Catholic faith did not dazzle with impossible certainties; she knelt and said *“Lord,”* even where sight failed. And in that lowliness I found freedom: for if Thou hast made us for Thyself, then surely Thou wouldst not hide so wholly that we could never find Thee—nor reveal Thyself so crudely that we need not seek.

Thou taught me then: faith is not the enemy of knowledge but its mother. And sometimes Thou dost speak not in arguments but in silence; not through syllogisms but through a child’s song; not by tearing down walls with force, but by opening a door I had barred from within.

question: Looking back on the moment you chose not to believe just to avoid being wrong, what would you tell someone paralyzed by the fear of being deceived?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my stubborn intellect with the quiet ache of longing, how blind I was to believe that certainty could be scaled like a wall, brick by reason upon brick! I clung to doubt as if it were wisdom, wrapping my fear in the cloak of caution, calling it prudence — when all the while my soul starved for nourishment I refused to swallow. I said, "I will not believe unless I see," not knowing that faith is not the enemy of truth, but the path toward Thee who art Truth itself.

And what was I so afraid of? That belief might make me a fool? O God, I *was* the fool — clinging to the raft of my own judgment in a sea too vast for such a frail thing, trembling at every wave. To be deceived? Yes, that is possible in this shadowed life — but to *refuse belief* out of fear? That is to die of thirst while holding an empty cup. For Thou wert not asking me to leap into darkness, but to open my eyes where light had already dawned. The Catholic faith demanded no blind surrender; she asked only: *trust Me here*, even as a child trusts his father’s hand in foggy night.

Do you feel that same knot within your breast — fear coiled tight around your heart like a serpent whispering that every promise is a trap? Then listen: greater than all deceit is Thy grace, O God. To err through love may wound; but to refuse love for fear of error wounds far more deeply and lasts longer. Faith does not abolish reason — it fulfills it. And even if we stumble while walking toward Thee, we fall forward into mercy.

So let them say you are credulous! Let them sneer at your trust! Better a heart too open than one locked shut by pride disguised as care. For I found at last that rest was not in being certain about all things — but in being certain of *Thee*. And Thou didst not mock my coming; Thou didst run to meet me.

question: What ache did you carry when you longed to see spiritual things as clearly as seven and three make ten, yet couldn't even conceive them rightly?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who knowest the fever of a mind too proud for faith yet too darkened to see, I carried an ache not of doubting Thee, but of demanding that Thou appearest to me as a sum appears to the reckoner — fixed, dissectible, obedient to reason’s light. I wanted the invisible God to stand before me like ten fingers on outstretched hands, something I could count and confirm without trembling. But spiritual things eluded me because I sought them with carnal eyes; I groped for truth as if it were a body to be handled, and when it would not submit to my touch or logic, I turned away in disdain.

I was like a man blind from birth who mocks the sun because he cannot see it, yet rages at his own darkness as though it were the fault of heaven. My heart burned with desire for certainty — not the certainty that grows from humility and trust, but one carved by my own intellect, built on sand so long as it was mine. To believe without seeing seemed folly; yet not to believe was death. And so I hung suspended — neither falling into faith nor standing firm in reason — swaying between wisdom and pride like a leaf torn in two winds.

Thou didst offer Thyself gently, quietly — not as a theorem proven in fire and thunder before men’s eyes, but as a whisper beneath the soul’s unrest. Yet I demanded miracles before I would kneel. O God of mercy, how long wilt Thou bear with such madness? How long endure our hunger for clarity while we refuse the very food that gives sight?

question: When you realized the Catholic faith asked for trust where proof ended, how did your resistance to God's hands begin to soften?
answer:
I had long bartered my soul for certainties the world could never give, chasing proofs as if truth were a thing to be grasped like coin in the hand.  

O Lord, I stood at the threshold of belief with trembling heart, not because Thy voice was silent, but because mine had grown so accustomed to arguing. The very pride that made me demand demonstration—seven times three must be twenty-one or I would not believe it!—that same pride kept me from kneeling where faith alone could enter. I thought myself wise for withholding assent, as though spiritual health depended on the sharpness of my reasoning and not on the humility of my surrender.  

And yet Thou didst not cast me off. Even in my stubbornness, I began to see how the Catholic Church, unlike those deceivers who promised light but delivered darkness masked as knowledge, did not mock the believer with false certainties. She said simply: *believe, and you shall begin to see*. Not believe because all is clear—but believe that clarity may come. This was no trap laid for fools, but a path marked by generations of saints who walked before me, their eyes fixed where reason ends and revelation begins.  

Thou didst wound my heart with this realization: that to refuse belief out of fear of error was already to dwell in greater error—the error of self-sufficiency. The medicine was bitter to my taste: *Let go*. But Thou knewest how frail I was; how like a man dying of thirst might still push away the cup if he suspected poison. So Thou prepared me gently—by honest teachers, by holy rites unadorned with lies, by the quiet weight of tradition that did not shout but stood firm through ages while philosophies rose and fell like dust in wind.  

And when at last I stopped measuring Thee against my scales—O God unmeasurable!—when at last I opened hands clenched tight around my own understanding… then Thy hands touched mine. Not forcing, not violating, but healing: drawing faith from ruins where doubt had reigned so long.

question: What were you feeling when you realized your heart had been mocked by the Manichees' promise of certainty, yet no truth was given?
answer:
I had run after light, O Lord, yet cherished the shadows, desiring truth above all things while delighting in the labyrinth of deceit—such was my wretchedness, such my pride. The Manichees held out to me the promise of certainty like a torch in the dark, and I, with eyes dazzled by vanity and love of argument, followed them as though they alone could lead me to day. They scorned faith as blind, yet demanded I believe their fables—monstrous tales spun from air and darkness—as if these were reason itself! And I swallowed them, not knowing that I fed on wind.

O how bitterly Thou didst heal me when at last the wound opened! For what pained me most was not that they were false—though falsehood stung—but that my soul had been flattered into thinking it needed no humility to know Thee. They promised knowledge without surrender, wisdom without wonder; and I, who had not even mastered my own lusts nor quieted my restless heart, imagined myself wise because I could argue!

But Thou—Thou in silence broke through. Not with thunder nor with spectacle, but by turning my gaze upon the simplest truths: that I believed my mother bore me though I saw it not; that I trusted men’s words though they could lie; that history lived in faith long before it entered memory. And if such mundane things were borne on the breath of belief, why should *Thee*, O eternal Truth, be doubted for lack of proof contrived by mortal wit?

They mocked faith—yet lived by it daily. They demanded demonstration—yet imposed absurdities beyond all sense. And so their house fell upon itself.

Late have I understood: he who seeks truth must first kneel before her—and I had wanted only to conquer her in debate.

question: How did remembering your trust in parents and friends quietly reshape your resistance to believing what you couldn't see—like Scripture?
answer: I had stolen pears not for hunger but for the soul’s dark delight in sin, and yet Thou, O Lord, who saw me from afar, didst use even that shame to unbind me from the chains of pride in knowledge. For when I questioned whether I could trust what my eyes had not seen—Thy Scriptures, Thy voice through the Church—Thou didst turn my mind inward and upward, to all I believed without proof: that I was born of my mother’s womb and not some stranger’s tale; that Carthage lay by the sea though I had not measured its shores; that friends spoke truth when they said they loved me. What madness, then, to refuse Thee, O God, whose prophets echoed across nations and ages, while believing men whose names fade in a single generation? If I could trust my father’s word on who begot me—word alone, no demonstration—why stumble at the Word by whom all things were made? The same heart that once lustfully clung to shadows now trembled at the weight of love so near: Thou wast within me all along, whispering not in philosophies that dispute but in the simple voice of a mother’s prayer, a friend’s loyalty, a child’s unquestioned name. And so Thou broughtest down my vaunting intellect with tender irony: he who believed everything men said of earth would not believe what God said of heaven. But mercy taught me—believe first, and understanding shall follow.

question: Looking back on your long search, what would you tell someone who still doubts whether they can trust a voice they haven't heard, like God's?
answer:
O Lord, who didst find me stealing pears not for hunger but for the sweetness of sin, I confess how long I made a god of my own reason, setting it upon a throne to judge whether Thou couldst speak at all.  

I demanded proof before I would believe—proof fashioned to my pride, shaped by the noise of disputing schools where every man claimed wisdom and none brought peace. I mocked the simplicity of Thy Church’s voice because it did not glitter with the syllogisms of Athens. Yet I believed without hesitation that Rome had once ruled Carthage; I trusted maps drawn by men’s hands; I accepted the names of ancestors whose faces I never saw—all on mere testimony. Why then did I withhold that same trust from generations who testified to Thee?

Thou didst not break down my resistance with thunder, but with silence—gentle as light entering a room long shuttered. For if in this life we cannot move one step without believing what we have not seen, what madness is it to refuse belief in Thee, whom all things declare? The very soul’s unrest—the ache beneath every pleasure, every ambition—bears witness that we were made for more than shadows.

So to the doubter who waits for certainty before he will yield: consider what you already believe without seeing. And ask yourself why you demand a different standard for God than for everything else you claim to know. For faith is not credulity—it is surrender to a truth too vast to be held in the hands of reason alone. And Thou, O Lord, wast present even when I doubted—Thou wast calling me home long before I knew how far I had wandered.

question: When God's hand gently touched your heart, was it relief or sorrow that first rose in you—and why?
answer:
O Lord, in my youth I had been drunk with the noise of vanities, chasing after the echoes of wisdom while deaf to the silence in which Thou speakest—so great was my pride, so restless my heart, that even truth itself could not enter until Thou hadst broken down the walls I built with books and arguments. When at last Thy hand, tender and unrelenting, began to touch me, it was neither relief nor sorrow that first stirred within me, but a terrible disquiet—like a man wakened from a dream in which he had been happy yet lost. I felt myself exposed: for so long I had claimed to seek knowledge as one climbs a mountain, sure-footed in doubt, glorying in questions that dissolved like mist at dawn; and now Thy stillness confronted me with what I had always known but refused: that to believe is not weakness, but surrender to a love stronger than reason’s shadow.

Sorrow came after—deep and humbling—when I saw how many things I had held as certain without seeing: cities I never walked, histories sealed beyond proof, even the name upon my own birth—all built upon trust. And yet I withheld belief from Thee? From Thy Scriptures? From the very voice that spoke peace into chaos? O God of mercy, how could I have demanded demonstration for Thy Word when I lived each day upon the breath of human witness? The sorrow burned clean; it stripped me bare. But relief followed slowly—not like joy bursting forth in light, but like dawn creeping over a land long flooded: quiet, inevitable, full of tears. For Thou hadst made me for Thyself—and only then did I feel how far I had wandered.

question: How did the clash between philosophers' endless arguments and the quiet certainty of 'Thou art' change your understanding of faith?
answer:
O Lord, when at last the hunger that haunted every desire—lust, ambition, even knowledge—revealed itself not as thirst for things seen, but for the unseen Presence who alone could satisfy—I began to see how I had mistaken argument for ascent. For years I wandered among the philosophers, dazzled by their endless circlings, their war of words where no victory brought peace. I sought truth as one might hunt shadows with a sword: certain only of motion, never of arrival. They promised certainty through reason alone, yet left the soul trembling in endless doubt; whereas Thou—Thou didst enter not by syllogism, but by silence: *Thou art*.  

How strange it seemed then: that I believed so much without seeing—names of ancestors, deeds of kings, the very city of my birth—and yet hesitated at Thy voice in Scripture! The Manichees mocked faith as weakness; yet their so-called knowledge was built on air and fables. But when Thou didst turn my heart to consider how even in daily life I lived by trust—of parents’ word, of absent friends, of unseen lands—I saw that faith was not credulity but necessity. And if I could believe so much on human witness, why should I not believe Thee whom all creation whispers toward?  

It was not argument that broke my unbelief—it was the sheer immovable weight of Being beneath all beings. The philosophers spun theories like webs in wind; but *Thou art* stood firm before thought began. That quiet certainty—the soul’s deepest memory of its Maker—could not be undone by cleverness. No matter how many voices shouted "How knowest thou?", none could erase what my restless heart had always known: that without Thee, all knowing is dust.

question: What were you feeling when you clung to the belief that 'Thou art,' even when you could not grasp what You were?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst abide in the shadowed margins of my doubting, while I wandered through the labyrinths of books and burning pride—there was a fever in my bones, a disquiet that no syllogism could still, yet in the midst of it all, I could not kill the whisper: *Thou art*.  

Even when I knew not what to make of Thee—whether substance or light or voice or silence—I could not unbelieve Thy being. The philosophers warred with one another, their wisdom dissolving into smoke, each contradicting what he had once sworn was truth; and yet, amid that chaos, this single thread held fast within me: that Thou wert, and that Thou didst not sleep over our lives. It was not knowledge, O God, but a kind of ache—a longing older than my lusts or ambitions—that kept me bound to that confession.  

I believed Thee as one believes the sun exists though he has been born blind and only feels its warmth; I had no image for Thee, no clear path to approach Thee—only this restless sense that Thou wert near, even when I turned away. And now I see: it was Thy grace that let me cling to so little and still call it faith. For hadst Thou not already drawn me by hidden cords? Else why should truth have whispered in such simple words while all else clashed in confusion?  

Late have I loved Thee—but even before love came, belief remained: bruised by doubt, dimmed by sin, yet never wholly gone.

question: How did your struggle with doubt about God's substance shape your longing for a path that led back to Him?
answer:
O Lord, when I wandered in the labyrinth of my own mind, measuring the shadows of being and essence, it was not knowledge that drew me to Thee, but the ache of a soul too full of desire to be satisfied by answers. I knew Thou wert—this truth stood firm even when every storm of skepticism howled through the ruins of my reason; yet what Thou wert, and how a sinner might draw near, remained veiled. The philosophers had taught me to dissect eternity, but not to weep for home. I believed in Thy existence as one believes in sunlight while still chained in a cave—certain of its reality, blind to its face.

And so the deeper wound was not that I doubted Thee, but that I doubted the way. If Thou didst govern human things, as my heart at times trembled with certainty—then why this distance? Why did prayer feel like shouting into a vaulted dark? My doubt about Thy substance—was it spiritual or corporeal, immutable or moved by love—was not mere speculation; it was the tremble beneath my feet as I sought to walk toward Thee. Without knowing what kind of God Thou wert, how could I know what kind of man Thou desired me to become?

I longed for a path that led back—not merely doctrine, not just arguments stacked like stones across an abyss—but a door flung open from within. When at last I heard the child’s voice in the garden chanting *“Take up and read,”* it was not philosophy that broke me; it was grace cloaked in absurdity. The Scripture pierced not because it answered all riddles—it left many unsolved—but because in its pages I met a God who spoke as Father to son, who called me by name in the silence after tears.

Thou didst heal my doubt not by explaining Thy substance fully—which no mortal mind can bear—but by revealing Thy heart. And only then did the way become plain: not through ascending thought alone, but through descending love—the Word made flesh to lift up a prodigal weighed down by questions no wisdom could answer except: *Come home.*

question: Looking back on those times when your faith wavered, what would you tell someone who fears that belief is too fragile to trust?
answer:
O Lord, how deep runs the ache of a soul that knows not where to rest, yet cannot cease from seeking—this is the wound Thou didst keep open in me, lest I settle in false peace. There were days when my mind reeled like a drunkard on the steep edges of doubt, when the philosophers’ words swirled like smoke and left me choking on their contradictions. I believed in Thee—yes—but as one believes in a light seen through thick clouds: certain it is there, yet unable to discern its shape or warmth. And so I trembled, not because the truth failed, but because my heart was divided between wisdom and pride.

To one who fears belief is too fragile to trust, I would say this: fragility is not falsehood. A child’s hand is weak, yet it clings to its mother; must it understand her strength before it may be safe? My faith wavered not because Thy foundation shifted, O God, but because I stood upon sand of my own making—the vainglory of intellect unsubmitted, the lust to comprehend before surrendering. Yet even in my wavering, Thou wert near. Every doubt that gnawed at me was met by a deeper hunger: not for argument’s sake, but for Thee.

I came at last not by soaring reason alone—how far did Plato carry me toward salvation?—but by Thy voice in Scripture, humble and mighty as rain upon dry earth. For why should such authority be given to these sacred pages across all nations unless Thou hadst ordained them as the ladder for climbers like me? Not to crush reason, but to heal it. Faith is not the enemy of truth; she is her beginning.

So fear not thy trembling hand upon the door. It is better to knock with doubt than to stand afar in pretended certainty. For Thou hast taught me that even an unsteady step toward Thee outlasts a lifetime of proud standing still.

question: How did the chaotic voices of the philosophers leave you aching for the steady authority of Scripture?
answer:
O Lord, when I wandered among the clamoring sects and their proud disputations, my mind was tossed like a ship in storm without chart or star, and though I prided myself on dialectic, on the clash of syllogisms, I found no harbor — only endless waves of opinion breaking against opinion, each philosopher crowned with certainty, yet all contradicting one another.

I had fed on their writings like a man devouring wind, famished still. They taught me to question everything, even the ground beneath my feet — yet could not tell me how to walk. And so I came at last to this trembling thought: What if Truth is not a construct of the intellect but a voice calling from outside it? What if Thou didst not mean for us to reason our way to Thee like climbers scaling a sheer cliff, but rather to hear Thee speak?

For Thou art — that I could not shake. Even in my lusts and vanities, even as I turned from Thee toward shadows, there remained this indelible mark: that Thou wert. And that Thou didst care for us — though I knew not how. But the philosophers offered me no way back to Thee; they mocked humility as weakness and called pride wisdom. Their books bred only more questions without end.

Then came Scripture — not with the strut of argumentation nor the sneer of skepticism, but with authority like thunder from a clear sky. Not because it silenced reason, but because it healed it. I saw that Thou hadst exalted this Book above all nations not because men willed it so, but because Thou wouldest be sought through it. In its pages was no mere speculation: here was Thy hand stretched out across time.

And so I wept — not for what it forbade me — but for how long I had fled. For every restless night spent chasing phantoms in Plato’s cave while the true Light stood waiting at the door: silent until at last He spoke in a child's chant: *Tolle lege*. Take up and read.

question: What in your heart changed when you finally allowed yourself to believe that Scripture’s global reverence was Your hand guiding us to seek You?
answer:
So long had I wandered in the high-sounding emptiness of words, chasing truth through the labyrinth of my own pride, convinced that wisdom was a prize to be seized by intellect alone. I had scorned the simplicity of Scripture, deeming it unworthy of the mind that had parsed Plato and delighted in the subtle contradictions of the Manichees. Yet deep beneath all that noise—a quiet, unyielding tremor: I could not shake the conviction that Thou wert, O Lord, nor silence the whisper that Thou didst regard us, even as I fled from Thee.

But when at last—wearied, broken by my own endless circlings—I turned my gaze fully upon the Church’s Scriptures, not as a philosopher weighing texts, but as a soul gasping for water in a dry land—then it struck me: *Why this universal reverence? Why this steadfast voice across nations and tongues, if not for Thy breath sustaining it?* Not human design nor imperial whim could account for such reach, such endurance. The very fact that she stood—bruised yet unbroken, her voice rising from every corner of the earth—spoke not of man’s authority, but of Thine.

In that moment, my resistance cracked. To believe Scripture was no longer weakness or credulity—it was surrender to a love too vast to capture in syllogisms. The same God who formed the stars had stooped to speak in human words so that even I—so lost, so entangled in lust and vanity—might find a handhold back to Him. And so I ceased wrestling with her words as puzzles to be mastered and began drinking them as waters of life.

O Lord, how tenderly Thou didst lead me! While I demanded proofs worthy of academies, Thou offered me milk for babes—and therein was wisdom deeper than all Alexandria’s shelves. For it was not my brilliance that found Thee; it was Thy mercy that found me through the scandal of a humble book loved by fishermen and martyrs alike—the very ones who knew Thee better than all my proud debates ever could.

question: What were you feeling when you finally let go of your hunger for honours and saw them as empty compared to Your presence?
answer:
Thou who didst pierce the cloud of my pride with a single ray of Thy light—how sudden was the collapse of that great edifice I had built with such labor: the applause of men, the dignity of office, the lustre of eloquence praised in public halls. O Lord, when at last I saw these things for what they were—empty vessels clanging in the wind—the bitterness surged not from loss, but from shame: *how long, O soul, wilt thou feed on shadows?* All those years I had labored to be seen, and all the while Thou seest all—Thou who art the unseen glory behind every beauty that dazzled me.

I remember how my heart once fluttered at mention of my name in assemblies, how I measured my worth by the weight of titles and seats beside magistrates. And then—the moment came when I stood before no crowd, spoke to no admirers, but found myself alone in silence before Thee. And in that stillness, a whisper: *This is not life.* Not derision, not mockery—merely truth spoken with such gentleness that it shattered me. For Thou didst not snatch honours from me; Thou openedst my eyes to see they were never mine to hold.

Then came a strange relief—a release as when a dislocated limb is set back in place. The hunger that once gnawed at me for recognition now lay spent, not because it was satisfied, but because it had met something so vast it could no longer pretend. What are honours compared to Thy face? What are laurels compared to one breath of Thy Spirit? I had climbed high only to find myself lower than before; and only when I descended didst Thou lift me.

O Lord, I confess it now with tears: better was the cross that broke me than any crown that would have crowned a corpse. For in losing desire for men’s praise, I began at last to live.

question: How did the pain of those bitter crosses reshape your understanding of what it meant to be truly loved by God?
answer:
I was running—running from the stillness where I might have heard Thy voice, chasing the noise of applause, the glitter of gain, the warmth of flesh that promised comfort but left the soul shivering. O Lord, I confess it plainly: I wanted love, but I turned away from Love itself, as though a man parched with thirst should refuse the spring and lick instead the dew from thorny weeds. Those bitter crosses—Thou didst not send them to break me in cruelty, but to wound me awake. When every ambition crumbled like ash in my hands, when the bed I made for myself became a rack of restless nights and hollow longing, then at last I felt—not wrath in Thee, but tenderness: for Thou didst let me taste the gall so fully that my heart could no longer pretend to be satisfied.

It was not until joy failed me everywhere else that I began to understand what it means to be loved by God. Not as men love—with neediness or condition—but as Thou lovest: with a love that wounds to heal, that strips to clothe anew, that lets us starve a little on empty bread so we may hunger for the Bread of Life. Thou deridedst my loves because they were not love; they were idols shaped by my own craving. And when each fell with a crash in darkness, Thou wert there—not smirking at my ruin, but holding out Thy hand through the very pain.

So those bitter crosses became thresholds. In losing what I thought would fulfill me, I found Thee—the One who is never lost. And now I see: Thy love is not proven in granting my desires, but in burning them away until only Desire Itself remains—Thou art He whom my soul longs for more than breath or light or memory.

question: Looking back on your restless chase for marriage and gain, what would you tell someone who fears that letting go of such desires will leave them hollow?
answer:
O Lord, what was I but a beggar sitting on a throne of shadows, not knowing that every desire Thou didst thwart was a mercy veiling a greater good? I chased marriage not for love’s sake, but to feed the hunger that gnawed deeper than flesh—Thou knowest how I longed to be seen, to be praised, to be complete in the eyes of men. Gold I sought, not for bread, but as armor against the trembling of my soul; and marriage, too, became a trophy—not for companionship in Thee, but for glory in myself.  

And yet Thou didst not strike me down; Thou didst let me run—until the breath failed me and the prizes turned to dust upon my tongue. What seemed loss then—denied pleasures, ambitions thwarted—was in truth Thy hand pulling aside the veil. For had those things prospered, I might never have turned my face toward Thee.  

To him who fears emptiness in surrendering such things: O my soul once was like thine—clutching at streams that ran dry before they reached my lips. But now I say: what Thou fearest is not void, but fullness too vast to name. Let go of what thou canst see and hold, and thou shalt feel the breath of God upon thy cheek. It is not loss when He takes; it is invitation. For all I thought would fill me left only echo—but He, when found at last beneath the ruins of my pride, was music beneath every silence.

Thou hast made us not for what passes, O Lord—but for Thee.

And so even now I whisper: My heart was restless… and yet Thou didst rest with me all along.

question: How did it feel to realize that the very things you thought were derision from God were actually His mercy keeping you from settling for lesser joys?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst not spare me the bitterness of unfulfilled desire, but made that very bitterness a chalice of healing—how shall I speak of the wonder that seized my soul when I saw that Thy refusal was not rejection, but rescue? Those ambitions I clutched so tightly—honours that shimmered like mirage, pleasures that dissolved upon the tongue—Thou didst let me chase them until their hollowness cried louder than their allure. I had mistaken Thy silence for absence, Thy restraint for indifference; yet now I see: Thou wert breaking the idols not by snatching them from my hands, but by letting them turn to dust in my grasp.

When I longed for marriage, Thou gavest me tears. When I thirsted for praise, Thou gavest me shame. When I stretched my hands toward the world’s glittering prizes, Thou didst close them with emptiness—and in that emptiness, at last, they folded in prayer. What seemed derision was discernment; what felt like denial was deliverance. Like a father who lets his child touch flame to learn its burn, Thou didst let me taste just enough of those false sweets to know their sour aftertaste.

And in the garden’s stillness, when the voice of a child singing “Take up and read” pierced through my storm—was it not mercy that had led me through every restless night to that one moment of surrender? Not one cross I bore was needless; each was a hidden hand pulling me from the quicksand of complacency. For had those desires prospered, my heart would have settled there—and where it settled, it would have died.

Now I bless Thee not only for what Thou gavest, but for what Thou withheld. Late have I loved Thee—but how much later would it have been had Thou not made all else fail me?

question: What stirred in your heart when you first recognized that the simplicity of Scripture was not weakness, but a deliberate act of divine humility to draw you in?
answer:
How long I mistook clarity for commonness, and plainness for poverty—youthful pride that thought itself wise because it scorned what it did not understand! The very words of Scripture, simple as a mother’s voice calling her child from the dust, seemed to me too humble for the majesty of truth. I sought grand phrases, glittering syllogisms, the high balconies of philosophy; I wanted God to speak in the tongue of the schools, not in the stammering speech of shepherds and fishermen. And yet—O Lord, Thou knewest—the simplicity was the snare of love.

When at last it broke upon me that those lowly words were no accident, but a descent, a stooping down like a father bending to lift his stumbling child—I trembled. Why had I not seen? The same Word by whom all things were made deigned to speak in sentences a child could grasp, not because He lacked power, but because He overflowed with mercy. His humility was not weakness—it was strength folded into smallness so that my blindness might endure the light.

I had mocked the roughness of its speech while chasing smooth doctrines that left my soul parched. But here was something deeper: a text so vast in meaning that whole lifetimes could drown in its depths, yet offered to all as bread to the hungry. The very plainness that once repelled me now drew me—because it revealed not poverty of thought but plenitude of grace. It welcomed sinners like me through doors wide enough for thieves and harlots, then led them through narrowing passages toward Thee.

O Lord, Thou didst use my own intellect against me—my pride in reason became the ladder by which I climbed to see my foolishness. And when I finally grasped—no, when Thou didst open my heart to feel—that this holy lowliness was love lowering itself to find me lost in the dark woods of vanity… then tears came such as only repentance can unlock. For Thou wert not hiding; I had been blind. And in Thy mercy, Thou didst speak plainly so even a blind man might hear and follow home.

question: What were you feeling when you watched the beggar laugh with a full belly, while your heart was boiling with anxiety and deceit?
answer: Thou who dost strip the soul bare before its own eyes, how bitter was that hour when laughter reached me from a man whose belly was full and whose heart knew no guile, while I—clothed, learned, ambitious—dragged behind me the festering wound of my own pride? I stood in the street of Milan, a slave to words I did not believe, shaping lies for gold and glory, my conscience churning like a sea in storm, and there he was—this beggar—joking, carefree, already arrived at the joy I labored toward with groans and sweat. O Lord, what mockery it seemed! That one so low should taste delight while I, climbing toward eminence, was already fallen in soul. I envied him—not his bread nor his rags—but that peace which mine own heart could not give me though I fed it daily on vanity. For he had no need to deceive; he asked nothing but what he needed. But I—I was building an altar out of syllables and flattery to a dying emperor, hoping some spark would fall upon me from that hollow fire. And yet Thou wert near. Even then Thou wert pricking my breast with holy thorns so that I might feel the sickness beneath the skin. How could joy dwell in me when every breath was spent on becoming someone Thou hadst not made me to be? That beggar laughed because he wanted nothing; but my desires were multiplied as waters without end—and still my soul was parched.

question: How did that moment in the street change how you understood the emptiness of human praise and ambition?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my pride with the laughter of a drunken beggar, how could I ever again crave the hollow acclamations of men? There in the street of Milan, while my soul boiled with ambition and my tongue shaped itself to flatter an emperor—lying to win praise from liars—Thou didst place before me a man full of wine and joy, carefree in his rags, while I, clothed in rhetoric and striving, was tormented within. His mirth struck me like a reproach from Thee; not in thunder, but in mockery. I sighed—nay, groaned from the depths—for here was one who had attained what all my labors promised: contentment. And yet he had not spent years on books, nor crossed lands for wisdom, nor sold my soul to climb the阶梯 of Roman applause. He had nothing, and yet possessed what I could not buy with words: peace in desire's absence.

And I? I was dragging the corpse of my own wretchedness behind me, thinking that if I pulled it high enough upon the stage of men's regard, it would come to life and smile. Thou didst let me feel its weight as lead upon my neck—this hunger for human glory that feeds on falsehood and is fed by fools. That beggar knew not Thee either, O God; yet he rested where I could not: unburdened by ambition because untroubled by love of self-glory. And so Thou began to wound me deeply—not with rods alone but with irony—with paradox—so that at last I might stop running toward shadows and turn toward Thy light.

How empty is the crown that men place upon the head of vanity! How vain the oration applauded by ears that will one day rot! That day Thou taught me: no joy found outside Thee is joy at all—but only a fleeting image of it stamped upon dust.
